Let's take a look at evidence from the accounting and consulting firm KPMG on how important for business a positive relationship can be. First, KPMG developed an algorithm to measure if the relationship between an account manager and a client was weak, moderately strong, or already strong. In other words, the extent to which the two people liked one another felt a sense of rapport with one another. Next, they looked to see whether the strength of the relationship influenced whether their account managers would get a yes from their clients after making a pitch for business. They found that when there was a non-existent or weak relationship with the client, the account manager would win the business only 30% of the time. However, account managers who had a relationship of moderate strength with their client got a yes 45% of the time. But the real traction came when the relationship was already strong. Under those conditions, the account manager got the win 65 to 70% of the time. So clearly, people prefer to do business inside good relationships. But in today's fast-paced, hyper-accelerated world, people will sometimes say that they don't have the time to build and nurture a relationship, that they're just too busy for it. Well, how long does it really take to strengthen a relationship? By using the three activators of liking that we will consider next, you can do it in less than 10 minutes.
